title: The Solar System
subject: Science
instructions: Work through the activities below. Use the Check button after each one — if you get stuck, a hint will help you.

sections.title: Warm-up: planet facts
sections.instructions: Quick questions to get you thinking about space.

activities.question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
activities.hint: It is also the smallest planet in the solar system.
activities.explanation: Mercury orbits closest to the Sun, at about 58 million kilometres.

activities.hint: Think about what the Sun is made of and what it gives out.
activities.explanation: False — the Sun is a star: a huge ball of burning gas that produces its own light.

activities.text: There are {{8|eight}} planets in our solar system, and they all orbit the {{Sun|sun}}.
activities.hint: Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
activities.explanation: Since 2006 the solar system officially has eight planets orbiting the Sun.

activities.prompt: Match each planet to its famous feature.
activities.hint: One of these features is a storm bigger than an entire planet.
activities.explanation: Saturn has the rings, Mars is red from iron oxide, Jupiter hosts the Great Red Spot, and Earth is the only one with liquid oceans.

activities.prompt: Put these four planets in order of distance from the Sun, nearest first.
activities.hint: The rocky planets are all nearer than the gas giants.
activities.explanation: Mercury (1st), Earth (3rd), Jupiter (5th) and Neptune (8th) from the Sun.

activities.prompt: If you could visit any planet, which one would you choose and why? Write two or three sentences.
activities.sampleAnswer: I would visit Saturn because I want to see its rings up close. Scientists say the rings are made of billions of pieces of ice and rock. I think flying over them would be the most beautiful view in the solar system.
activities.hint: Pick the planet first, then give two reasons — one fact you know, one thing you would love to see.
